original_abstract: To improve the synthesis of ammonia, a molecule of critical industrial importance, a deep mechanistic understanding of its catalytic production from dinitrogen is essential. In this study, we investigate the synthesis of ammonia using samarium diiodide (SmI₂) and water as a reductant/proton source in the presence of molybdenum complexes featuring N-heterocyclic carbene-based PCP-type pincer ligands. Our kinetic analysis reveals that the rate-determining step shifts depending on catalyst concentration: at high concentrations, the reaction is limited by the proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) involved in N–H bond formation at the molybdenum imide center, whereas at low concentrations, catalyst dimerization becomes the bottleneck. Guided by density functional theory (DFT) calculations, we designed and synthesized a series of PCP-type pincer ligands with varying electronic properties at the 5- and 6-positions. We found that introducing electron-withdrawing groups significantly enhances the reaction rate by stabilizing key intermediates and improving the catalyst's electron affinity. Specifically, a molybdenum trichloride complex bearing a trifluoromethyl-substituted ligand emerged as an exceptionally robust catalyst. It achieved an unprecedented turnover number of up to 60,000 equivalents of ammonia per molybdenum atom, with a turnover frequency reaching 800 equivalents per minute. These results represent a significant leap in catalytic efficiency and offer a promising framework for the development of sustainable, next-generation nitrogen fixation technologies.
humanized_abstract: To design better catalysts for the production of the economically and socially important ammonia (NH3) molecule, it is necessary to have a thorough understanding of catalytic dinitrogen reduction mechanisms. The production of ammonia using samarium diiodide (SmI2) as a catalyst in conjunction with water as a reductant/proton source was studied with a molybdenum (Mo) precursor in the presence of N-heterocyclic carbene (NHC)-based PCP (pyridine-corrole-pyridine)-type pincer ligands. Analysis of kinetic profiles for the ammonia production reaction indicated that the rate-determining step of the catalytic cycle varied depending on the catalyst concentration. At high concentrations of the molybdenum complex, the PCET step associated with N-H bond formation to the molybdenum imide center was found to be the rate-determining step in the cycle; at low concentrations, however, the catalyst dimerization step became the rate-determining step in the cycle. Based on this finding, the design and synthesis of a series of ligands, composed of PCP-type pincer ligands and variations at the 5- and 6-positions of the corrole component using various combinations of electron-donating and electron-withdrawing groups is described. Based on DFT calculations that described how each of the ligands affected the stability of species and the electron affinity of the resulting complexes, catalyst precursors containing electron-withdrawing groups provided higher turnover rates than those with electron-donating substituents. Of the complexes examined, that of a trifluoromethyl substituted corrole showed the highest activity. The trifluoromethyl-corrole complex catalyst precursor gave an unprecedented TON value of up to 60,000 ammonia equivalents of per Mo and a TOF of 800 min(-1).
